title: Today's AI Brief: Uber's AI Budget Caps and New Server Hopes
summary: Uber caps employee AI spending, a new server aims to break AI's 'memory wall'
imageAlt: Image from source article for Uber caps employee AI spending after blowing through budget in 4 months
imageCaption: Uber caps employee AI spending after blowing through budget in 4 months
imageSourceTitle: Uber caps employee AI spending after blowing through budget in 4 months

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to today's AI Brief. I've got a couple of interesting stories to share with you. First up, Uber has put a cap on employee AI spending after blowing through their budget in just four months. I guess you could say they got a little too excited about AI - like that one friend who gets a bit too enthusiastic at the buffet. It's a good reminder that AI can be expensive, and companies need to keep an eye on their spending.

According to TechCrunch, Uber's AI spending got out of hand quickly. The company didn't disclose the exact budget or the new cap, but it's clear they're trying to get a handle on costs. AI can be expensive, especially when you're using it for things like customer service chatbots or optimizing routes. My read is that Uber's move shows just how quickly AI can eat into budgets if not managed carefully. It's not hard to see why - AI models require massive amounts of data, computing power, and memory.

Also making headlines is a new server designed to tackle AI's 'memory wall'. This refers to the challenge of handling the massive amounts of data needed for large language models. The idea is that this new server will help make AI more efficient by providing more memory. It's a bit like upgrading your computer's RAM, but on a much larger scale. This could be a game-changer for companies working with AI, as it could enable them to build more complex models and train them more quickly.

My quick read: the artificial intelligence story keeps getting bigger, but also stranger in very human ways. One moment it is strategy and infrastructure; the next, the Vatican is in the room. That is a pretty good sign this technology has left the lab and wandered into the town square.
